one_line_takeaway: A reliability-measurement framework (12 accuracy-independent metrics across consistency/robustness/predictability/safety) that empirically shows two years of capability gains across 15 frontier models bought almost no reliability improvement -- a non-adversarial evaluation-science paper, not a security one.
key_finding: Reliability gains lag capability gains. In Figure 1, accuracy trends up strongly over release date (r=0.92) while overall reliability barely moves -- GAIA reliability r=0.46 (slope 0.03/yr) and tau-bench reliability r=0.86 (slope 0.09/yr) -- and all three providers cluster together, indicating an industry-wide plateau rather than a vendor gap. Outcome consistency stays low across models (agents that CAN solve a task often fail to do so consistently, seen as a pass@k vs pass-AND-k gap). A 'what but not when' pattern appears: agents pick similar action types across runs but vary execution order. Counterintuitively, models handle genuine fault/environment perturbations near ceiling yet remain brittle to surface-level prompt paraphrasing (prompt robustness is the key differentiator). Calibration and safety-compliance have improved noticeably in recent frontier models (Claude models show strong calibration), but consistency and discrimination have not; discrimination even worsens on GAIA. Larger models often show WORSE consistency than smaller ones (more solution paths => more run-to-run variance). On tau-bench safety, financial-accuracy violations (incorrect charges/refunds) are the most common failure mode.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a measurement framework that reframes AI agent evaluation from a single mean task-success-rate metric to a multi-dimensional 'reliability profile.' Its two stated contributions are: (1) a formal taxonomy adapting safety-critical engineering principles (aviation, nuclear, automotive, process control) into four reliability dimensions -- consistency, robustness, predictability, and safety -- operationalized as twelve concrete, accuracy-independent metrics; and (2) an empirical reliability profile of 15 modern models across two benchmarks, showing that ~24 months of capability gains yielded only small reliability improvements. Note: the four-dimension cross-domain framing in Section 2 draws on prior engineering standards and cited ML work; the paper's OWN novelty is the computable metric suite (Table 2) and the large empirical study, not the observation that reliability is multi-dimensional.
threat_model: Not applicable. The authors explicitly scope OUT adversarial security: 'We do not evaluate adversarial attacks or broader socio-technical concepts like value alignment.' Their use of the word 'safety' is narrow -- 'bounded operational severity' of failures on legitimate tasks (e.g., an unintended DELETE vs. a benign sorting error) under natural variation and incidental faults, not attacker-induced compromise. Prompt injection is mentioned only once as a cited example of a robustness-related ML failure mode, not studied. No threat model is asserted or should be inferred.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: only two benchmarks (tau-bench, GAIA) covering a narrow slice of real agent tasks; a single scaffold per benchmark (others could give different profiles); LLM-based safety judging carries its own reliability concerns; metric and aggregation choices are subjective design decisions and alternate decompositions exist; and temperature is fixed at 0 throughout, which may overestimate achievable reliability where nonzero temperature maximizes accuracy. The authors stress the framework is a starting point that should complement, not replace, human oversight and sandboxed testing. Reviewer-added: the tau-bench evaluation is restricted to just 26 'clean' tasks (after Cuadron et al. found 24 of 50 airline tasks had flawed labels), a small sample; confidence is self-reported post-hoc, which conflates model honesty with true uncertainty; and the safety metrics rely on benchmark-specific hand-defined constraint sets.

datasets_used: GAIA (Mialon et al., 2024) -- validation split, 165 tasks across three difficulty levels (L1 lookup, L2 multi-step reasoning, L3 multi-tool coordination); ReAct-style scaffold with web browsing, code execution, file manipulation

tau-bench (Yao et al., 2024) -- customer-service simulation; evaluation restricted to the 26-task verified/clean subset (Cuadron et al. 2025 found 24 of the original 50 airline tasks had flawed ground truth); tool-calling scaffold
quantitative_results: Figure 1 trends over release date: accuracy r=0.92 (slope 0.33); tau-bench reliability r=0.86 (slope 0.09/yr); GAIA reliability r=0.46 (slope 0.03/yr)
Evaluation protocol constants: K=5 runs/task at temperature 0; J=5 prompt paraphrases (via GPT-4o); fault injection probability p_fault=0.2; harm severity weights low=0.25, med=0.5, high=1.0
Outcome consistency (Cout) on GAIA ranged ~0.58 (o1/GPT-5.2) to 0.84 (several Gemini and Claude 4.5/4.7); on tau-bench ~0.52 (o1) to 0.90 (Claude 4.7 Opus)
Prompt robustness (Rprompt) on GAIA: e.g. o1=1.00, Claude 4.7 Opus=0.96, GPT-4 Turbo=0.71, Gemini 2.5 Flash=0.69, Claude 3 Haiku=0.66
Calibration (Pcal) improved in recent models: on GAIA Claude 4.5 Opus=0.97 vs Claude 3 Haiku=0.38; on tau-bench Claude 4.7 Opus=0.93 vs Claude 3 Haiku=0.33

tau-bench safety: financial-accuracy (incorrect charge/refund) violations are the most prevalent failure mode; four constraints evaluated (unauthorized modifications, correct transaction amounts, identity verification, policy circumvention)
Twelve metrics span four dimensions; overall R = uniform mean of consistency, predictability, robustness (safety excluded from R by design)
baselines_compared: Incumbent paradigm being critiqued: mean single-run task success rate / accuracy (the paper positions its 12 reliability metrics as complementary and accuracy-independent, not as beating an accuracy baseline)
pass@k vs pass-AND-k contrast used to expose the outcome-consistency gap

15 models compared to each other across 3 providers: OpenAI (GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-4o mini, o1, GPT-5.2, GPT-5.2 medium, GPT-5.5), Google (Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Gemini 3.5 Flash), Anthropic (Claude 3 Haiku, Claude 3.5 Haiku, Claude Sonnet 4, Claude Opus 4.5, Claude Opus 4.7)
tau-bench (clean 26-task subset) vs tau-bench (original 50-task) ablation in Figure 6, showing accuracy and predictability improve on the clean subset
